title: XCITE Center for Teaching and Learning Dashboard on Academic Technology Usage

University of California, Riverside

Gathering data from academic learning tool vendors
Cleaning, preparing, and organizing data into a database
Analyzing and presenting the analysis in a data reporting tool through dashboards
Disseminating the insights and key metrics to supervisors and stakeholders

University of California, Riverside

Gathering data from academic learning tool vendors
Cleaning, preparing, and organizing data into a database
Analyzing and presenting the analysis in a data reporting tool through dashboards
Disseminating the insights and key metrics to supervisors and stakeholders

title: Conferences & Presentations
text:
Washington, D.C. — April 12, 2025


This study estimates the extent of education–occupation mismatch and the associated wage penalties for undocumented college graduates. Using data from the American Community Survey (ACS), we classify workers as vertically mismatched (higher educational attainment than is typical for the occupation) or horizontally mismatched (field of degree is not typical for the occupation). Because the ACS does not identify undocumented status, we train machine learning models on the Survey of Income and Program Participation (SIPP) and use the predictions to impute status in the ACS. This approach enables new analyses of labor market outcomes for undocumented college graduates in nationally representative surveys. Results indicate that undocumented status is linked to higher rates of both vertical and horizontal mismatch, as well as wage penalties of roughly 4–7 percent. These penalties are smaller among the DACA-eligible and in states with more inclusive immigrant policy climates.





 Download PDF

This approach enables new analyses of labor market outcomes for undocumented college graduates in nationally representative surveys. Results indicate that undocumented status is linked to higher rates of both vertical and horizontal mismatch, as well as wage penalties of roughly 4–7 percent. These penalties are smaller among the DACA-eligible and in states with more inclusive immigrant policy climates.

title: Conferences & Presentations
text:
Abstract
This study estimates the extent of education–occupation mismatch and the associated wage penalties for undocumented college graduates. Using data from the American Community Survey (ACS), we classify workers as vertically mismatched (higher educational attainment than is typical for the occupation) or horizontally mismatched (field of degree is not typical for the occupation). Because the ACS does not identify undocumented status, we train machine learning models on the Survey of Income and Program Participation (SIPP) and use the predictions to impute status in the ACS. This approach enables new analyses of labor market outcomes for undocumented college graduates in nationally representative surveys. Results indicate that undocumented status is linked to higher rates of both vertical and horizontal mismatch, as well as wage penalties of roughly 4–7 percent. These penalties are smaller among the DACA-eligible and in states with more inclusive immigrant policy climates.

title: Data Drift Analysis with CatBoost
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(DBI)
library(RPostgres)
library(arrow)


# --- 1. FETCH ACS DATA ---
# --- 1. FETCH ACS DATA (Direct Connection Workaround) ---
#con <- dbConnect(
  #RPostgres::Postgres(),
  #dbname = "postgres",
  #host = "aws-1-us-east-2.pooler.supabase.com", 
  #port = 6543,
  #user = "postgres.dpuhypyqwupzesfkiplu",
  #password = Sys.getenv("SUPABASE_PWD"),
  #connect_timeout = 10
#)

#acs_foreign_summary <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM acs_catboost_summary")
#dbDisconnect(con)


acs_foreign_summary <- read_parquet("acs_catboost_summary_rows.parquet")

# --- 2. LOAD DHS CSVs ---
dhs_male <- read_csv("Unauthorized Immigrant Population Estimates (Male) 2009-2022 - Unauthorized Immigrant Population Estimates (Male) 2009-2022.csv") %>%
  filter(Year >= 2009 & Year <= 2019) %>%
  rename(year = Year, age_bracket = Age, population_count = Count) %>%
  mutate(gender = "Male")

dhs_female <- read_csv("Unauthorized Immigrant Population Estimates (Female) 2009-2022 - Unauthorized Immigrant Population Estimates (Female) 2009-2022.csv") %>%
  filter(Year >= 2009 & Year <= 2019) %>%
  rename(year = Year, age_bracket = Age, population_count = Count) %>%
  mutate(gender = "Female")

dhs_combined <- bind_rows(dhs_male, dhs_female) %>%
  mutate(
    age_bracket = str_trim(str_replace_all(age_bracket, '=|"', '')),
    dataset = "DHS Official"
  ) %>%
  filter(age_bracket != "Under 18", age_bracket != "Total", !is.na(year))

# --- 3. PREP ACS GENDER DATA ---
acs_combined <- acs_foreign_summary %>%
  # Translate ACS sex codes to match DHS text
  mutate(
    gender = case_when(
      fem == 1 ~ "Female",
      fem == 0 ~ "Male",
      TRUE ~ "Unknown"
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(age_bracket != "Under 18", gender != "Unknown", !is.na(year)) %>%
  group_by(year, age_bracket, gender) %>%
  #Change this back to recall 
  summarise(population_count = sum(high_recall_count, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(dataset = "Imputed ACS")



# --- 4. MERGE AND APPLY THE OFFSET TRICK ---
plot_data <- bind_rows(dhs_combined, acs_combined) %>%
  mutate(
    age_bracket = factor(age_bracket, levels = c("18-24", "25-34", "35-44", "45-54", "55 And Over")),
    gender = factor(gender, levels = c("Male", "Female")),
    
    # Create the interaction grouping for colors
    fill_group = paste(gender, dataset, sep = " - "),
    
    # THE OFFSET TRICK: Shift ACS slightly left, DHS slightly right
    age_numeric = as.numeric(age_bracket),
    plot_x = ifelse(dataset == "Imputed ACS", age_numeric - 0.2, age_numeric + 0.2)
  )

title: Data Drift Analysis with CatBoost
text:
Code
library(tidyverse)
library(DBI)
library(RPostgres)
library(arrow)


# --- 1. FETCH ACS DATA ---
# --- 1. FETCH ACS DATA (Direct Connection Workaround) ---
#con <- dbConnect(
  #RPostgres::Postgres(),
  #dbname = "postgres",
  #host = "aws-1-us-east-2.pooler.supabase.com", 
  #port = 6543,
  #user = "postgres.dpuhypyqwupzesfkiplu",
  #password = Sys.getenv("SUPABASE_PWD"),
  #connect_timeout = 10
#)

#acs_foreign_summary <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM acs_catboost_summary")
#dbDisconnect(con)


acs_foreign_summary <- read_parquet("acs_catboost_summary_rows.parquet")

# --- 2. LOAD DHS CSVs ---
dhs_male <- read_csv("Unauthorized Immigrant Population Estimates (Male) 2009-2022 - Unauthorized Immigrant Population Estimates (Male) 2009-2022.csv") %>%
  filter(Year >= 2009 & Year <= 2019) %>%
  rename(year = Year, age_bracket = Age, population_count = Count) %>%
  mutate(gender = "Male")

dhs_female <- read_csv("Unauthorized Immigrant Population Estimates (Female) 2009-2022 - Unauthorized Immigrant Population Estimates (Female) 2009-2022.csv") %>%
  filter(Year >= 2009 & Year <= 2019) %>%
  rename(year = Year, age_bracket = Age, population_count = Count) %>%
  mutate(gender = "Female")

dhs_combined <- bind_rows(dhs_male, dhs_female) %>%
  mutate(
    age_bracket = str_trim(str_replace_all(age_bracket, '=|"', '')),
    dataset = "DHS Official"
  ) %>%
  filter(age_bracket != "Under 18", age_bracket != "Total", !is.na(year))

# --- 3. PREP ACS GENDER DATA ---
acs_combined <- acs_foreign_summary %>%
  # Translate ACS sex codes to match DHS text
  mutate(
    gender = case_when(
      fem == 1 ~ "Female",
      fem == 0 ~ "Male",
      TRUE ~ "Unknown"
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(age_bracket != "Under 18", gender != "Unknown", !is.na(year)) %>%
  group_by(year, age_bracket, gender) %>%
  #Change this back to recall 
  summarise(population_count = sum(high_recall_count, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(dataset = "Imputed ACS")



# --- 4. MERGE AND APPLY THE OFFSET TRICK ---
plot_data <- bind_rows(dhs_combined, acs_combined) %>%
  mutate(
    age_bracket = factor(age_bracket, levels = c("18-24", "25-34", "35-44", "45-54", "55 And Over")),
    gender = factor(gender, levels = c("Male", "Female")),
    
    # Create the interaction grouping for colors
    fill_group = paste(gender, dataset, sep = " - "),
    
    # THE OFFSET TRICK: Shift ACS slightly left, DHS slightly right
    age_numeric = as.numeric(age_bracket),
    plot_x = ifelse(dataset == "Imputed ACS", age_numeric - 0.2, age_numeric + 0.2)
  )

title: Data Drift Analysis with CatBoost
text:
2. Calculating Distribution Metrics (18-54 Population)


Code
# 1. Filter to 18-54 and Recalculate 100% Denominators
plot_data_filtered <- plot_data %>%
  filter(year != 2013) %>%
  filter(age_bracket %in% c("18-24", "25-34", "35-44", "45-54")) %>%
  group_by(year, dataset) %>%
  mutate(
    total_pop_year = sum(population_count, na.rm = TRUE),
    percentage = population_count / total_pop_year
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# 2. Recalculate Metrics (PSI, KS) for the new 18-54 Universe
metrics_calc <- plot_data_filtered %>%
  group_by(year, age_bracket, gender, dataset) %>%
  summarise(percentage = sum(percentage, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = dataset, values_from = percentage) %>%
  rename(P_ACS = `Imputed ACS`, Q_DHS = `DHS Official`) %>%
  mutate(
    P_ACS = if_else(is.na(P_ACS) | P_ACS == 0, 0.00001, as.numeric(P_ACS)),
    Q_DHS = if_else(is.na(Q_DHS) | Q_DHS == 0, 0.00001, as.numeric(Q_DHS))
  )

final_metrics <- metrics_calc %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    PSI = sum((P_ACS - Q_DHS) * log(P_ACS / Q_DHS)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  left_join(
    metrics_calc %>%
      group_by(year, age_bracket) %>%
      summarise(P_ACS_Age = sum(P_ACS), Q_DHS_Age = sum(Q_DHS), .groups = "drop") %>%
      group_by(year) %>%
      arrange(year, age_bracket) %>%
      mutate(CDF_ACS = cumsum(P_ACS_Age), CDF_DHS = cumsum(Q_DHS_Age), abs_diff = abs(CDF_ACS - CDF_DHS)) %>%
      summarise(Max_CDF_Diff_Age = max(abs_diff), .groups = "drop"),
    by = "year"
  )

title: Data Drift Analysis with CatBoost
text:
3. Distribution Shift Metrics Over Time


Code
library(ggplot2)

# Pivot metrics for plotting
metrics_long <- final_metrics %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(PSI, Max_CDF_Diff_Age),
    names_to = "Metric",
    values_to = "Value"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Metric = case_when(
      Metric == "Max_CDF_Diff_Age" ~ "Max CDF Difference (KS Proxy)",
      TRUE ~ Metric
    )
  )

# Plot the trendlines
p_metrics_trend <- ggplot(metrics_long, aes(x = year, y = Value, color = Metric, group = Metric)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2009:2012, 2014:2019)) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Max CDF Difference (KS Proxy)" = "#BA4A00", "PSI" = "#5D3A9B")) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution Shift Metrics Over Time",
    subtitle = "Tracking the stability of the imputed ACS distribution against DHS official estimates",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Metric Score",
    color = "Metric"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )

p_metrics_trend









Code
ggsave("Distribution_Metrics_Trend.png", plot = p_metrics_trend, width = 10, height = 5, dpi = 300)

title: Data Drift Analysis with CatBoost
text:
4. Horizontal Distribution Overlay


Code
library(scales)

# 1. Configure the Scorecards
scorecards <- final_metrics %>%
  mutate(
    score_text = sprintf("PSI: %.3f\nMax CDF Diff.: %.3f", PSI, Max_CDF_Diff_Age),
    x_pos = 4.5, 
    y_pos = Inf  
  )

# 2. Aggregate Genders & Apply Offset Logic
plot_data_bars <- plot_data_filtered %>%
  group_by(year, dataset, age_bracket) %>%
  summarise(percentage = sum(percentage, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(
    age_numeric = as.numeric(factor(age_bracket, levels = c("18-24", "25-34", "35-44", "45-54"))),
    plot_x = ifelse(dataset == "Imputed ACS", age_numeric - 0.2, age_numeric + 0.2)
  )

# 3. Generate the Final Plot (Horizontal Facets)
p_bars_final <- ggplot(plot_data_bars, aes(x = plot_x, y = percentage, fill = dataset)) +
  
  geom_col(width = 0.4, color = "black", alpha = 0.9) +
  
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(percentage, accuracy = 0.1)), 
            vjust = -0.5, size = 3, fontface = "bold") +
  
  geom_label(
    data = scorecards,
    aes(x = x_pos, y = y_pos, label = score_text),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    hjust = 1, vjust = 1.2,
    fill = "gray98", color = "black", fontface = "bold", size = 3, alpha = 0.9, 
    label.padding = unit(0.3, "lines"), label.size = 0.3
  ) +
  
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("DHS Official" = "#7FB3D5", "Imputed ACS" = "#E66100")) +
  
  # Increased the expansion on the right side to 40% to comfortably fit the scorecard in smaller panels
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:4, labels = levels(factor(plot_data_bars$age_bracket)), expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.40))) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(), expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.25))) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Age Distribution (18-54 Population): Imputed ACS vs. DHS Official",
    subtitle = "Sequential year-over-year comparison",
    x = "Age Bracket",
    y = "Percentage of Filtered Population",
    fill = "Dataset"
  ) +
  
  # THE HORIZONTAL FIX: ncol = 5 splits the 10 years into 2 rows of 5 panels
  facet_wrap(~ year, ncol = 5) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "top", 
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    panel.spacing = unit(0.5, "lines"), 
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12, hjust = 0), 
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.margin = margin(10, 10, 10, 10) 
  )

p_bars_final









Code
ggsave("Distribution_Overlay_Bars_Horizontal.png", plot = p_bars_final, width = 16, height = 8, dpi = 300)

title: Data Drift Analysis with CatBoost
text:
5. Vertical Distribution Overlay


Code
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(scales)
library(tidyr)

# ==============================================================================
# 1. Filter to 18-54 and Recalculate 100% Denominators
# ==============================================================================
plot_data_filtered <- plot_data %>%
  filter(year != 2013) %>%
  # Drop out-of-sample ages to strictly match the SIPP training domain
  filter(age_bracket %in% c("18-24", "25-34", "35-44", "45-54")) %>%
  
  # Recalculate the denominator so these 4 brackets equal exactly 100%
  group_by(year, dataset) %>%
  mutate(
    total_pop_year = sum(population_count, na.rm = TRUE),
    percentage = population_count / total_pop_year
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# ==============================================================================
# 2. Recalculate Metrics (PSI, KS) for the new 18-54 Universe
# ==============================================================================
metrics_calc <- plot_data_filtered %>%
  group_by(year, age_bracket, gender, dataset) %>%
  summarise(percentage = sum(percentage, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = dataset, values_from = percentage) %>%
  rename(P_ACS = `Imputed ACS`, Q_DHS = `DHS Official`) %>%
  mutate(
    P_ACS = if_else(is.na(P_ACS) | P_ACS == 0, 0.00001, as.numeric(P_ACS)),
    Q_DHS = if_else(is.na(Q_DHS) | Q_DHS == 0, 0.00001, as.numeric(Q_DHS))
  )

scorecards <- metrics_calc %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    # Dropped KL Divergence; focusing entirely on the symmetric PSI
    PSI = sum((P_ACS - Q_DHS) * log(P_ACS / Q_DHS)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  left_join(
    metrics_calc %>%
      group_by(year, age_bracket) %>%
      summarise(P_ACS_Age = sum(P_ACS), Q_DHS_Age = sum(Q_DHS), .groups = "drop") %>%
      group_by(year) %>%
      arrange(year, age_bracket) %>%
      mutate(CDF_ACS = cumsum(P_ACS_Age), CDF_DHS = cumsum(Q_DHS_Age), abs_diff = abs(CDF_ACS - CDF_DHS)) %>%
      summarise(Max_CDF_Diff_Age = max(abs_diff), .groups = "drop"),
    by = "year"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    score_text = sprintf("PSI: %.3f\nMax CDF Diff.: %.3f", PSI, Max_CDF_Diff_Age),
    # Pushed safely to the far right, past the 4th age bucket
    x_pos = 4.5, 
    y_pos = Inf  
  )

# ==============================================================================
# 3. Aggregate Genders & Apply Offset Logic
# ==============================================================================
plot_data_bars <- plot_data_filtered %>%
  # Aggregate male/female so we only plot one total bar per age bracket
  group_by(year, dataset, age_bracket) %>%
  summarise(percentage = sum(percentage, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(
    age_numeric = as.numeric(factor(age_bracket, levels = c("18-24", "25-34", "35-44", "45-54"))),
    # Offset by half the bar width so they sit perfectly flush
    plot_x = ifelse(dataset == "Imputed ACS", age_numeric - 0.2, age_numeric + 0.2)
  )

# ==============================================================================
# 4. Generate the Final Plot
# ==============================================================================
p_bars_final <- ggplot(plot_data_bars, aes(x = plot_x, y = percentage, fill = dataset)) +
  
  # A. The Bars: Added color = "black" for outlines, width perfectly matches offset
  geom_col(width = 0.4, color = "black", alpha = 0.9) +
  
  # B. Bar Height Labels: Applied to BOTH datasets
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(percentage, accuracy = 0.1)), 
            vjust = -0.5, size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
  
  # C. Scorecard Annotation
  geom_label(
    data = scorecards,
    aes(x = x_pos, y = y_pos, label = score_text),
    inherit.aes = FALSE,
    hjust = 1, vjust = 1.2,
    fill = "gray98", color = "black", fontface = "bold", size = 3.5, alpha = 0.9, 
    label.padding = unit(0.4, "lines"), label.size = 0.3
  ) +
  
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("DHS Official" = "#7FB3D5", "Imputed ACS" = "#E66100")) +
  
  # Added a 15% expansion on the right side of the x-axis to make room for the scorecard
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:4, labels = levels(factor(plot_data_bars$age_bracket)), expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.15))) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(), expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.25))) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Age Distribution (18-54 Population): Imputed ACS vs. DHS Official",
    x = "Age Bracket",
    y = "Percentage of Filtered Population",
    fill = "Dataset"
  ) +
  
  facet_wrap(~ year, ncol = 1) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "top", 
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    panel.spacing = unit(0.2, "lines"), 
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12, hjust = 0), 
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5) 
  )

p_bars_final









Code
ggsave("Distribution_Overlay_Bars_Final.png", plot = p_bars_final, width = 8, height = 18, dpi = 300)

title: Mario Arce Acosta
text:
Hello, nice to meet you! My name is Mario, and I am currently studying Statistics through the Master’s in Science program at the University of California, Davis. I also received my Bachelor’s in Science in Applied Mathematics/Economics and a Minor in Business Analytics from the University of California Riverside.
My special interests are machine learning, labor economics, and the role/experience of undocumented immigrants in the economy. I am currently conducting research on the labor market outcomes of undocumented college graduates using machine learning imputation methods.
I am proud to say that I have presented my research (coauthored with Dr. Veronica Sovero at UC Riverside) at national conferences such as at the Population Association of America (PAA) 2025 Annual Conference and the 2025 Association for Public Policy Analysis & Management (APPAM) Conference!

title: US Labor & Immigration Demographics
text:
This dashboard pulls a pre-aggregated decade of US Census microdata from a cloud PostgreSQL database to visualize macroeconomic shifts in the labor market.

I managed the pipeline from raw vendor extracts to cleaning hundreds of thousands of rows in Google Sheets. title: Exploratory Data Analysis of DHS Estimates
text: Before deploying any advanced modeling, it is critical to understand the shape, missingness, and distributions of the raw Department of Homeland Security data. The primary objective for this post is to examine the demographic distribution of the DHS estimates from 2009-2019, specifically focusing on the age and gender composition of the undocumented population over the decade.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis of DHS Estimates
text: Before deploying any advanced modeling, it is critical to understand the shape, missingness, and distributions of the raw Department of Homeland Security data. The primary objective for this post is to examine the demographic distribution of the DHS estimates from 2009-2019, specifically focusing on the age and gender composition of the undocumented population over the decade.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis of DHS Estimates
text:
Loading the Data
We’ll start by pulling in the summary statistics and estimates from our database and our extracted DHS tables. Data preparation can be described in two steps:

Training machine learning models on cleaned SIPP data to impute undocumented status in the ACS using gradient-boosted trees. This process is identical to what can be found here.
Creating a high-level snapshot of this data to be uploaded via SQL to Supabase for future use, alongside our extracted official DHS demographic tables.



Code
library(tidyverse)
library(DBI)
library(RPostgres)

# --- 1. FETCH ACS DATA ---
con <- dbConnect(
  RPostgres::Postgres(),
  dbname = "postgres",
  host = "aws-1-us-east-2.pooler.supabase.com", 
  port = 6543,
  user = "postgres.dpuhypyqwupzesfkiplu",
  password = Sys.getenv("SUPABASE_PWD"),
  connect_timeout = 10
)
acs_foreign_summary <- dbGetQuery(con, "SELECT * FROM acs_foreign_summary")
dbDisconnect(con)

# --- 2. LOAD DHS CSVs ---
dhs_male <- read_csv("Unauthorized Immigrant Population Estimates (Male) 2009-2022 - Unauthorized Immigrant Population Estimates (Male) 2009-2022.csv") %>%
  filter(Year >= 2009 & Year <= 2019) %>%
  rename(year = Year, age_bracket = Age, population_count = Count) %>%
  mutate(gender = "Male")

dhs_female <- read_csv("Unauthorized Immigrant Population Estimates (Female) 2009-2022 - Unauthorized Immigrant Population Estimates (Female) 2009-2022.csv") %>%
  filter(Year >= 2009 & Year <= 2019) %>%
  rename(year = Year, age_bracket = Age, population_count = Count) %>%
  mutate(gender = "Female")

dhs_combined <- bind_rows(dhs_male, dhs_female) %>%
  mutate(
    age_bracket = str_trim(str_replace_all(age_bracket, '=|"', '')),
    dataset = "DHS Official"
  ) %>%
  filter(age_bracket != "Under 18", age_bracket != "Total", !is.na(year))

# --- 3. PREP ACS GENDER DATA ---
acs_combined <- acs_foreign_summary %>%
  # Translate ACS sex codes to match DHS text
  mutate(
    gender = case_when(
      fem == 1 ~ "Female",
      fem == 0 ~ "Male",
      TRUE ~ "Unknown"
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(age_bracket != "Under 18", gender != "Unknown", !is.na(year)) %>%
  group_by(year, age_bracket, gender) %>%
  #Change this back to recall 
  summarise(population_count = sum(high_recall_count, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(dataset = "Imputed ACS")



# --- 4. MERGE AND APPLY THE OFFSET TRICK ---
plot_data <- bind_rows(dhs_combined, acs_combined) %>%
  mutate(
    age_bracket = factor(age_bracket, levels = c("18-24", "25-34", "35-44", "45-54", "55 And Over")),
    gender = factor(gender, levels = c("Male", "Female")),
    
    # Create the interaction grouping for colors
    fill_group = paste(gender, dataset, sep = " - "),
    
    # THE OFFSET TRICK: Shift ACS slightly left, DHS slightly right
    age_numeric = as.numeric(age_bracket),
    plot_x = ifelse(dataset == "Imputed ACS", age_numeric - 0.2, age_numeric + 0.2)
  )

title: Exploratory Data Analysis of DHS Estimates
text:
Age & Gender Composition
To establish a solid demographic baseline, we examine the age and gender composition of the undocumented population reported by DHS over the years 2009-2019. By stacking the male and female estimates, we can simultaneously observe the total size of each age bracket alongside its internal gender distribution.

Faceted/Overview Plots of DHS Demographics


Code
library(ggplot2)
library(scales) 
library(plotly)

p_comparison_faceted <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = plot_x, y = population_count, fill = fill_group)) +
  # Because the X coordinates are physically separated, "stack" will only stack 
  # Male on top of Female within the same dataset!
  geom_col(position = "stack", color = "black", alpha = 0.9, width = 0.35) +
  
  # Assign distinct colors to distinguish ACS from DHS visually
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Male - DHS Official"   = "#2471A3", # Dark Blue
    "Female - DHS Official" = "#7FB3D5", # Dark Red
    "Male - Imputed ACS"   = "#BA4A00", # Light Blue
    "Female - Imputed ACS" = "#F5B041"  # Light Red
  )) +
  
  # Map the numeric X-axis back to the categorical Age Bracket text
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = 1:5,
    labels = levels(plot_data$age_bracket)
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_comma()) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Population Estimates by Age and Gender: Imputed ACS vs DHS Official",
    x = "Age Bracket",
    y = "Total Population Estimate",
    fill = "Dataset & Gender"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, color = "black"),
    panel.border = element_rect(color = "gray80", fill = NA),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    
    # 2. Force extra space between the facet panels
    panel.spacing = unit(2, "lines"), 
    # Make the year titles a bit bolder to stand out
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", margin = margin(b = 10, t = 10)) 
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ year, ncol = 3)

# 3. Give the ggplotly conversion a slight margin push for its own legend
ggplotly(p_comparison_faceted, tooltip = c("fill", "y")) %>%
  layout(legend = list(orientation = "h", x = 0.5, xanchor = "center", y = -0.1), margin = list(b = 60))








Year-by-Year Interactive Composition


Code
library(plotly)

p_interactive_comparison <- plot_ly(
  data = plot_data,
  x = ~plot_x,
  y = ~population_count,
  color = ~fill_group,
  colors = c(
    "Male - DHS Official"   = "#2471A3", 
    "Female - DHS Official" = "#7FB3D5", 
    "Male - Imputed ACS"   = "#BA4A00", 
    "Female - Imputed ACS" = "#F5B041"
  ),
  frame = ~year,          
  type = 'bar', 
  text = ~dataset,
  hovertemplate = '<b>%{text}</b><br>%{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>' 
) %>%
  layout(
    barmode = 'stack',    
    
    title = list(
      text = "<b>Interactive Comparison: Imputed ACS vs DHS Official</b>",
      font = list(family = "Arial", size = 16),
      x = 0.5 
    ),
    
    # Map the numeric Plotly axis back to text labels
    xaxis = list(
      title = "<b>Age Bracket</b>",
      tickvals = 1:5,
      ticktext = levels(plot_data$age_bracket),
      tickfont = list(size = 12)
    ),
    
    yaxis = list(
      title = "<b>Total Population Estimate</b>",
      tickformat = ",d", 
      range = c(0, max(
        plot_data %>% 
          group_by(year, plot_x) %>% 
          summarise(total = sum(population_count, na.rm=TRUE), .groups="drop") %>% 
          pull(total)
      ) * 1.1)
    ),
    
    # 1. Push the legend further down (from -0.25 to -0.5 or -0.6)
    legend = list(
      orientation = "h",  
      x = 0.5,            
      xanchor = "center",
      y = -0.5            
    ),
    
    # 2. Increase the bottom margin significantly (from 100 to 160)
    margin = list(b = 160) 
  ) %>%
  animation_opts(
    frame = 1200,         
    transition = 500,     
    redraw = FALSE        
  ) %>%
  animation_slider(
    currentvalue = list(prefix = "<b>Year:</b> ", font = list(color = "black")),
    pad = list(t = 40)
  )

p_interactive_comparison

title: Exploratory Data Analysis of DHS Estimates
text:
Summary
From these simple comparisons we can notice that our GBM imputations appears to overestimate the number of undocumented immigrants in age brackets 18-24 and 25-34, and underestimate undocumented immigrants in age brackets 35-44 and 45-54.
